segments.text: Part 1A: Water, pH, buffers Chapter 2 Dr. Rizk IUSB

2.1 What Are the Properties of Water?
Water has unusual properties:
High b.p., m.p., heat of vaporization, surface tension
Bent structure makes it polar
H-bond donor and H-bond acceptor
Potential to form four H-bonds per water molecule

Cohesive and adhesive properties
Hydrogen bonding gives rise to the cohesive and adhesive properties of water
Cohesive
Water sticking
to itself
Adhesive
Water sticking to
another material

The Solvent Properties of Water Derive from Its Polar Nature
Ions are always hydrated in water and carry around a "hydration shell"
Water forms H bonds with polar solutes

Hydrophobic Interactions
A nonpolar solute "organizes" water
The H-bond network of water reorganizes to accommodate the nonpolar solute
Example: fatty acid
This is an increase in "order" of water resulting in a decrease in ENTROPY
More favorable
entropically
Entropy is related to the degrees of freedom in a system

Hydrophobic Interactions
Water in bulk solution can constantly break and re-form H-bonds with other water molecules
Surrounding a hydrophobic molecule, water is “trapped” in specific configuration = fewer degrees of freedom = lower entropy
More favorable
entropically
Entropy is related to the degrees of freedom in a system

Entropy of water drives hydrophobic interactions
Figure 2.6 Nonpolar molecules decrease the entropy of solvent water (left). When nonpolar molecules coalesce (arrow), the entropy of the solvent increases.
More favorable:
Fewer waters with low entropy

The Solvent Properties of Water Derive from Its Polar Nature
Figure 2.7 (a) Sodium palmitate is an amphiphilic molecule.

Micelle formation
Figure 2.7 (b)  Micelle formation by amphiphilic molecules in aqueous solution.
Hydrophobic core

2.1 What Are the Properties of Water?
Water Can Ionize to Form H + and OH -
H 2 O ⇄ H + + OH -

2.2 What is pH?
Søren Sørensen of Denmark devised the pH scale
pH is the negative logarithm of the hydrogen ion concentration
If [H + ] = 1 x 10 -7 M
Then pH = 7
2.2 What is pH?
2.2 What is pH?

Review Acids and Bases
The Brønsted–Lowry definition is most commonly used:
A Brønsted–Lowry acid is a proton (H + ) donor .
A Brønsted–Lowry base is a proton (H + ) acceptor .
H 3 O + ( aq )   +   Cl − ( aq )
HCl( g )   +   H 2 O( l )
This proton is donated.
HCl is a Brønsted–Lowry acid .
H 2 O is a Brønsted–Lowry base .
H 2 O accepts the proton.

A. Brønsted–Lowry Acids
A Brønsted–Lowry acid must contain a hydrogen atom .
Common Brønsted–Lowry acids ( H A):

B. Brønsted–Lowry Bases
A Brønsted–Lowry base is a proton acceptor , so it must be able to form a bond to a proton.
A base must contain a lone pair of electrons that can be used to form a new bond to the proton.

Dissociation of Weak Electrolytes
Consider a weak acid, HA
What is the acid dissociation constant equation?
HA ⇄ H + + A -
HA: conjugate acid (protonated form)
A - : conjugate base (de-protonated form)

The Henderson-Hasselbalch Equation
Know this! You'll use it constantly .
For any acid HA, the relationship between the pK a , the concentrations existing at equilibrium and the solution pH is given by:
HA ⇄ H + + A -
The pH is related to the pKa of the acid and the ratio of conj. base to conj. acid

Example
What is the pH of a solution containing 0.36 M sodium acetate and 0.72 M acetic acid? Note: the pKa of acetic acid is 4.8
Acetic acid: CH3COOH		 (conj. acid)
Sodium acetate: CH3COO- Na+  (conj. base)
pH = 4.8 + log (0.36/0.72) = 4.5
Even having twice as much acid as base changed the pH by 0.3 points!

Example
What is the pH of a solution containing the same concentration of conjugate acid and conjugate base?
[A - ] = [HA]
[A - ]/[HA] = 1
pH = pKa + log (1)      what’s log (1)?
pH = pKa
pKa is the pH when [HA] = [A-]
Zero!

2.2  What is pH?
Titration curves illustrate the progressive dissociation of a weak acid
Little change in pH

2.3  What Are Buffers, and What Do They Do?
Buffers are solutions that resist changes in pH as acid and base are added
Most buffers consist of a weak acid and its conjugate base
Note in Figure 2.14 how the plot of pH versus base added is flat near the p K a
Buffers can only be used reliably within a pH unit of their p K a

2.3  What Are Buffers, and What Do They Do?
Figure 2.14  A buffer system consists of a weak acid, HA and its conjugate base, A -
Strong base
weak base
Weak acid
strong acid
Adding a strong base (OH-), results in forming a weak base (A-)
Adding a strong acid (H+), results in forming a weak acid (HA)

Imidazole
